title: Dhvanyāloka [Sahṛdayāloka]
author: Ānandavardhana
genre: philosophical treatise on poetics (kārikā with vṛtti)
summary: The foundational treatise of the dhvani ('suggestion/resonance') school of Sanskrit literary theory. It argues that the soul of poetry (kāvya) is not its literal sense or its ornaments but the suggested meaning (vyaṅgyārtha), realized especially as suggested aesthetic mood (rasa-dhvani). The work systematically classifies types of suggestion and elevates suggested rasa above figures of speech and qualities.
history: Composed by Ānandavardhana, a court poet under King Avantivarman of Kashmir; the title Sahṛdayāloka ('Light for the Connoisseur') is sometimes used. The work transformed Sanskrit poetics by shifting focus to inner meaning, and was given its most influential elaboration in Abhinavagupta's commentary, the Locana. Whether the kārikās and vṛtti are by one or two hands has been debated.

title: Kāvyālaṃkāra
author: Bhāmaha
genre: philosophical treatise on poetics (śāstra in verse)
date_estimate: c. 7th c. CE (disputed; some place late 7th-early 8th c.)
summary: One of the earliest extant systematic treatises on Sanskrit poetics, in six chapters (paricchedas) totaling about 398 verses. It treats the nature and aims of poetry, poetic faults (doṣa), figures of speech (alaṃkāra), and correctness of language, and is foundational to the alaṃkāra ('ornament') school. Bhāmaha treats kāvya as a discipline separate from dramaturgy.
history: Bhāmaha, probably a Kashmiri, is a key early authority often discussed alongside (and in rivalry with) Daṇḍin; their relative dating is debated. His emphasis on vakrokti (striking/oblique expression) as central to alaṃkāra influenced later theorists such as Udbhaṭa and Kuntaka.

title: Candrāloka
author: Jayadeva Pīyūṣavarṣa
genre: philosophical treatise on poetics (didactic verse)
summary: A popular, elementary verse handbook of Sanskrit poetics in ten chapters (mayūkhas), totaling about 350 verses. Written in a lucid, mnemonic style, it covers poetic faults, qualities, and especially figures of speech, often illustrating each device with an example that itself embodies the device. Its arrangement reworks the topic-sequence of Mammaṭa's Kāvyaprakāśa.
history: By Jayadeva, styled Pīyūṣavarṣa ('rain of nectar'), not to be confused with the Gītagovinda poet Jayadeva. Its popularity is attested by numerous later commentaries (e.g., by Gāgābhaṭṭa, Vaidyanātha). It became a standard teaching text in traditional pāṭhaśālā curricula.

title: Rasagaṅgādhara
author: Jagannātha Paṇḍitarāja
genre: philosophical treatise on poetics
summary: A large, sophisticated treatise on poetics that reopens and refines the central questions of rasa, dhvani, and the definition of poetry using the analytical rigor of Navya-Nyāya logic. Jagannātha offers a fresh definition of kāvya as language whose meaning is charming (ramaṇīyārtha) and critically engages the entire prior tradition, especially Mammaṭa and Appayya Dīkṣita. The work is incomplete, breaking off in its treatment of figures.
history: Composed by Jagannātha Paṇḍitarāja (c. 1590-1670), a Telugu Brahmin scholar-poet who received the title Paṇḍitarāja at the Mughal court of Shah Jahan. He is traditionally regarded as the last great luminary of classical Sanskrit poetics, and the Rasagaṅgādhara is its culminating analytical work.

title: Kāvyālaṃkāra-sūtra-vṛtti
author: Vāmana (NOT Bhāmaha; metadata author hint appears incorrect)
genre: philosophical treatise on poetics (sūtra with author's own vṛtti)
summary: A treatise composed as aphorisms (sūtras) with the author's own prose gloss (vṛtti), best known for the doctrine that rīti (a particular structured arrangement of words, i.e., 'style/diction') is the soul of poetry. It treats poetic qualities (guṇa), faults, and figures, grounding aesthetic value in the choice and arrangement of words.
history: Despite the project metadata attributing this to Bhāmaha, the Kāvyālaṃkārasūtravṛtti is the well-known work of Vāmana, the great exponent of the rīti (style) school and a contemporary of Udbhaṭa, traditionally placed around 800 CE and associated with the court of King Jayāpīḍa of Kashmir. It is regarded as an early systematic philosophy of literary aesthetics.

title: Vakroktijīvita
author: Kuntaka (Rājānaka Kuntaka)
genre: philosophical treatise on poetics (kārikā with author's vṛtti)
summary: A major treatise arguing that the essence ('life', jīvita) of all literature is vakrokti, 'oblique/striking expression'—the creative deviation of poetic language from ordinary usage. Kuntaka develops an elaborate six-fold typology of vakratā ('crookedness/strikingness') ranging from the level of phonemes and words up to whole works, presenting it as a unifying principle of poetic beauty.
history: Kuntaka, a Kashmiri theorist usually dated between Ānandavardhana (9th c.) and Abhinavagupta (10th-11th c.), reworked Bhāmaha's notion of vakrokti into a comprehensive aesthetic. The text survives incompletely; it was critically edited by S. K. De (1923/1928). It represents an alternative to the dominant dhvani school.

title: Kāvyaprakāśa
author: Maṃmaṭa (Mammaṭa)
genre: philosophical treatise on poetics (kārikā with vṛtti)
summary: The single most influential and widely taught compendium of Sanskrit poetics, in ten chapters (ullāsas) of verse kārikās with prose explanation and illustrative examples. It synthesizes the dhvani (suggestion) and alaṃkāra (ornament) traditions, treating the nature and purpose of poetry, word-functions (abhidhā, lakṣaṇā, vyañjanā), faults, qualities, figures, and the classification of poetry by suggestion.
history: By Mammaṭa, a Kashmiri scholar of the late 11th-12th c.; the final portion is traditionally said to have been completed by a second hand (Allaṭa/Alaka). It became the standard textbook of poetics, attracting over fifty commentaries—reputedly second only to the Bhagavadgītā among Sanskrit works in number of commentaries.

title: Kāvyaprakāśa
author: Maṃmaṭa (Mammaṭa)
genre: philosophical treatise on poetics (kārikā with vṛtti)
summary: A second digital recension/edition of Mammaṭa's Kāvyaprakāśa, the canonical ten-chapter (ullāsa) handbook of Sanskrit poetics. It defines poetry and its aims, analyzes the three word-functions and the mechanism of suggestion (dhvani), and classifies poetic faults, qualities, and figures of speech, synthesizing the dhvani and alaṃkāra schools.
history: Same work as GK16mamkavpu; this work_id likely encodes a different edition or manuscript version (e.g., text-plus-commentary base). Mammaṭa was an 11th-12th c. Kashmiri theorist, and the work spawned the largest commentarial tradition in Sanskrit poetics.

title: Śabdavyāpāravicāra
author: Maṃmaṭa (Mammaṭa)
genre: philosophical treatise (short monograph on semantics/poetics)
summary: A short specialized treatise ('Inquiry into the Operations of the Word') by Mammaṭa setting out the semantic functions of language—denotation (abhidhā), indication (lakṣaṇā), and suggestion (vyañjanā)—and defending the independence of vyañjanā as the basis of poetic suggestion. It argues against critics (notably Mukula Bhaṭṭa) who tried to reduce suggestion to the other functions.
history: A lesser-known companion piece to Mammaṭa's Kāvyaprakāśa, concentrating on the theory of word-power that underlies the dhvani doctrine. It is an important source for the Sanskrit philosophy of language as applied to poetics.

title: Kāvyamīmāṃsā
author: Rājaśekhara
genre: philosophical treatise on poetics (practical handbook, partly in sūtra style)
summary: A practical handbook (originally planned in eighteen sections, of which the first, the Kavirahasya, survives) on the making of a poet. It discusses the poet's training, talent (pratibhā) and learning, the literary 'assembly', plagiarism, regional styles, and the geography and conventions (kavi-samaya) of poetic composition rather than abstract aesthetics.
history: By Rājaśekhara, a poet-dramatist and court scholar of the Pratihāra kings Mahendrapāla and Mahīpāla. The Kāvyamīmāṃsā is unusual in its sociological and pedagogical orientation and is a valuable source on the poetic culture and literary geography of early medieval India.

title: Nāṭakacandrikā
author: Rūpa Gosvāmī
genre: treatise on dramaturgy
summary: A treatise ('Moonlight of Drama') on the rules of dramaturgy, summarizing classical Sanskrit nāṭya theory (dramatic types, plot structure, sentiments) and adapting it to Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava devotional drama. It provides the technical framework underlying Rūpa's own devotional plays on Kṛṣṇa.
history: By Rūpa Gosvāmī (c. 1489-1564), one of the Six Gosvāmins of Vṛndāvana and a leading theologian of the Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava movement founded by Caitanya. The work supports his project of recasting classical rasa-dramaturgy in the service of bhakti, as realized in his plays Vidagdha-mādhava and Lalita-mādhava.

title: Vyaktivivekavyākhyā
author: Rājānaka Ruyyaka
genre: bhasya/commentary (vyākhyā on a poetics treatise)
summary: A commentary by Ruyyaka on Mahima Bhaṭṭa's Vyaktiviveka. The base text (Vyaktiviveka) argues against the dhvani theory, holding that what others call 'suggestion' is really a form of inference (anumāna), so that rasa is inferred rather than directly manifested; Ruyyaka's vyākhyā systematically expounds and evaluates these positions.
history: Ruyyaka, a 12th-century Kashmiri theorist and teacher (reputedly of Maṅkha), was among the first to reflect systematically on Mahima Bhaṭṭa's notion of vyakti ('manifestation'). The commentary is an important witness to the debate between the dhvani school (Ānandavardhana, Abhinavagupta) and its critics.

title: Alaṅkārasarvasva
author: Rājānaka Ruyyaka
genre: philosophical treatise on poetics (sūtra/kārikā with author's vṛtti)
summary: A systematic treatise ('The Whole Treasury of Ornament') devoted chiefly to the classification and analysis of figures of speech (alaṃkāra). Ruyyaka treats roughly eighty-two figures—a small number of word-figures (śabdālaṃkāra) and the majority sense-figures (arthālaṃkāra)—defining and exemplifying each within a dhvani-informed framework.
history: By Rājānaka Ruyyaka, a 12th-century Kashmiri scholar writing some two and a half centuries after Ānandavardhana. The work became a standard reference on figures and is best known together with the elaborate commentary (Vimarśinī) of Jayaratha. (Authorship has occasionally been linked to Maṅkha, Ruyyaka's pupil.)

title: Alaṅkārasarvasva
author: Rājānaka Ruyyaka
genre: philosophical treatise on poetics (sūtra/kārikā with vṛtti)
summary: Another recension/edition of Ruyyaka's Alaṅkārasarvasva, a treatise focused on the systematic classification of figures of speech (alaṃkāras), with definitions and illustrative examples set within a suggestion-oriented theory of poetry. This work_id likely corresponds to a particular edition or accompanying commentary version.
history: Same work as GK16ruyalscu; multiple work_ids reflect different editions/manuscripts (notably text with or without Jayaratha's or Samudrabandha's commentary). Ruyyaka is a 12th-century Kashmiri theorist; the text is a key authority on alaṃkāra in the post-Mammaṭa period.

title: Alaṅkārasarvasva
author: Rājānaka Ruyyaka
genre: philosophical treatise on poetics (sūtra/kārikā with vṛtti)
summary: A further recension/edition of Ruyyaka's Alaṅkārasarvasva, the standard 12th-century treatise on figures of speech. It defines and exemplifies the inventory of śabdālaṃkāras and arthālaṃkāras and situates the analysis of ornament within the dhvani tradition. The 'vsu' suffix in the work_id probably marks a version carrying a particular commentary (e.g., Samudrabandha's vṛtti).
history: Same underlying work as GK16ruyalscu/GK16ruyalssu, transmitted with different commentaries (Jayaratha's Vimarśinī in Kashmir; Samudrabandha's in the South). Ruyyaka's treatise became the principal medieval reference work on alaṃkāra.

title: Rasārṇavasudhākara
author: Siṃhabhūpāla (Singabhūpāla / Recherla Siṅgama Nāyaka)
tradition: Hindu; Sanskrit poetics and dramaturgy (rasa/nāṭya theory)
genre: treatise on rasa and dramaturgy
summary: A treatise ('Moon to the Ocean of Aesthetic Sentiment'), arranged in three 'rays' (vilāsas), giving an exhaustive account of rasa theory—the sentiments, their determinants and consequents, the hero and heroine typology (nāyaka-nāyikā-bheda), states of love, and dramatic conventions. It draws heavily on the Nāṭyaśāstra and Daśarūpaka traditions and is widely cited by later writers.
history: By Siṃhabhūpāla, a king of the Recherla (Velama) line in Andhra, a learned patron also credited with the music treatise Saṅgītasudhākara, active around 1330. His work on rasa is quoted by the commentator Mallinātha, attesting its early authority. Critically edited by T. Venkatacharya (Adyar Library, 1979).

title: Rasārṇavasudhākara
author: Siṃhabhūpāla (Singabhūpāla / Recherla Siṅgama Nāyaka)
tradition: Hindu; Sanskrit poetics and dramaturgy (rasa/nāṭya theory)
genre: treatise on rasa and dramaturgy
summary: A second recension/edition of Siṃhabhūpāla's Rasārṇavasudhākara, the three-part (vilāsa) compendium on aesthetic sentiment and dramaturgy. It systematizes rasa, bhāva, the hero-heroine typology, and the conventions of Sanskrit drama, synthesizing the Nāṭyaśāstra and Daśarūpaka traditions.
history: Same work as GK16simhrs_u; the distinct work_id reflects a separate edition or manuscript version of the text. Composed by the 14th-century Andhra ruler-scholar Siṃhabhūpāla and cited by Mallinātha.

title: Kāvyālaṃkārasārasaṃgraha
author: Udbhaṭa (Bhaṭṭa Udbhaṭa)
genre: philosophical treatise on poetics (kārikā compendium)
summary: A concise compendium ('Collection of the Essence of the Ornaments of Poetry') treating about forty-one figures of speech (alaṃkāras), word- and sense-based, organized in six groups, each defined and illustrated. It refines and partly responds to Bhāmaha's treatment of figures and adds new ones (e.g., punaruktavadābhāsa, kāvyahetu, kāvyadṛṣṭānta).
history: By Udbhaṭa, a Kashmiri poetician traditionally said to have been a court scholar (sabhāpati) of King Jayāpīḍa of Kashmir, and a contemporary of Vāmana. The work is a key link between the early (Bhāmaha) and later phases of the alaṃkāra school; it survives with the commentary of Pratīhārendurāja.

title: Kāvyālaṃkārasārasaṃgraha
author: Udbhaṭa (Bhaṭṭa Udbhaṭa)
genre: philosophical treatise on poetics (kārikā compendium)
summary: Another recension/edition of Udbhaṭa's Kāvyālaṃkārasārasaṃgraha, a compact treatise on figures of speech arranged in six groups (about 41 alaṃkāras), each defined and exemplified. This work_id likely corresponds to the text accompanied by Pratīhārendurāja's commentary (Laghuvṛtti).
history: Same work as GK16udkass_u; differing work_ids reflect distinct editions or text-plus-commentary versions. Udbhaṭa, a late-8th-century Kashmiri scholar at Jayāpīḍa's court, bridges the early and classical phases of the ornament school.

title: Ujjvalanīlamaṇi
author: Rūpa Gosvāmī (Rūpa Gosvāmin)
genre: philosophical/theological treatise on devotional aesthetics
summary: A specialized treatise ('The Blazing Blue Gem') that elaborates madhura-rasa, the 'sweet' erotic-devotional sentiment of love between Kṛṣṇa and the gopīs (especially Rādhā), which Rūpa had treated only briefly in his Bhakti-rasāmṛta-sindhu. Applying classical rasa categories (vibhāva, anubhāva, sthāyibhāva) to bhakti, it analyzes the heroines, the divine lovers, their moods, and the structure of sacred amorous emotion.
history: By Rūpa Gosvāmī (c. 1489-1564), a foremost theologian among the Six Gosvāmins of Vṛndāvana in the Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava tradition of Caitanya. It is the definitive Gauḍīya text on madhura-rasa and was provided with influential commentaries (e.g., by Jīva Gosvāmī and Viśvanātha Cakravartī).
related_works: Locanarocanī / commentaries by Jīva Gosvāmī and Viśvanātha Cakravartī
